summary: Explore 'Explore Circuit installation/setup UX options now that the Claude Code plugin may delegate to an external circuit-next binary. Compare self-contained plugin, plugin plus separate CLI install, npm-packaged CLI dependency, bootstrap installer, and development-only wrapper paths. Weigh tradeoffs for first-run success, maintenance, release/versioning, host compatibility, and long-term product shape. Recommend a path.': Recommend a phased path: ship a plugin-plus-separate-CLI install with an npm-packaged CLI as the canonical distribution channel, gated behind a bootstrap check inside the plugin that detects a missing/outdated circuit-next binary and prints a single copy-pasteable install command (npm i -g circuit-next or equivalent). Rationale across the five options: (1) self-contained plugin keeps first-run success highest but blocks the architectural move to an external binary and forces every runtime change through a plugin re-release, which the brief signals is the direction of travel; (2) plugin + separate CLI install with no helper has the worst first-run UX (two-step install, version-skew confusion) and should be avoided as the default; (3) npm-packaged CLI dependency gives the cleanest release/versioning story (semver, lockfiles, global or per-project install, works on macOS/Linux/Windows where Node is already a Claude Code prerequisite, so host compatibility is effectively free) and matches how Claude Code itself is distributed; (4) bootstrap installer (curl|sh or plugin-invoked downloader) improves first-run success but introduces a second supply-chain surface, platform-specific binary builds, and an upgrade path the plugin must own forever — acceptable as a fallback but not the primary channel; (5) dev-only wrapper is fine for the inner loop but cannot be the user-facing answer. The phased path: phase 1 keep the wrapper for development; phase 2 publish circuit-next to npm and have the plugin detect-and-prompt; phase 3 evaluate a bootstrap installer only if telemetry shows the npm prompt is a real drop-off. Evidence is thin: I did not inspect the current plugin entry points, the circuit-next binary's build/packaging, or any telemetry on first-run failure rates. Confirmed facts from the provided reports are limited to the brief itself; everything about Claude Code's Node prerequisite, plugin release cadence, and host coverage is assumption-level until verified against scripts/runtime/, the plugin manifest, and the circuit-next package layout. Trustworthy proof would require: (a) confirming circuit-next can be built as a Node-resolvable package (or a prebuilt-binary npm package), (b) confirming the plugin host can shell out to a globally-installed CLI on all supported platforms, (c) a first-run script that exercises the detect-and-prompt path on a clean machine, and (d) a versioning policy doc stating how plugin and CLI semver are coupled.

review_fold_ins.overall_assessment: Compose stays on-subject, ranks all five named options against the brief's five axes (first-run success, maintenance, release/versioning, host compatibility, long-term product shape), and lands on a phased recommendation that is internally consistent with its own ranking. Epistemic calibration is appropriate: the answer explicitly flags that evidence is thin, separates the brief-confirmed facts from assumptions about Claude Code's Node prerequisite and circuit-next packaging, and names four concrete proofs (package build check, cross-platform shell-out check, clean-machine first-run script, versioning policy doc) that would move the recommendation from assumption-grounded to evidence-grounded. evidence_refs cite only reports/brief.json and reports/analysis.json, which are the two real artifacts provided in this run; nothing fabricated. success_condition_alignment is substantive — it names the five comparison axes, the assumption/fact split, and the four specific proofs, so it would not paste cleanly into an unrelated compose.
missed_angles: Licensing/distribution implications of publishing circuit-next to npm (package name squatting, org ownership, public vs. scoped) are not weighed, which can constrain the npm-as-canonical-channel recommendation.
Offline/air-gapped and corporate-proxy install scenarios are not considered, which materially affect whether npm-global is a viable default channel for some Claude Code users.

No discussion of how the plugin detects an outdated (vs. merely missing) circuit-next and whether the version-skew prompt is silent on match, which is the part of the detect-and-prompt UX most likely to regress first-run success.
